result:
Lately I’ve been thinking about the idea of “in-between moments,” the spaces in life that often go unnoticed. These intervals aren’t exactly dramatic milestones, nor are they particularly dull. They exist somewhere in the middle: waiting at a bus stop as the sky shifts color, walking a familiar block, stirring a pot of soup, closing a browser tab. They carry the faint hum of routine and transience. It’s interesting to consider how much of our lives happens in these spaces, invisible parts that rarely make it into our stories or recollections. We usually remember the big announcements, the celebrations, the heartbreaks, but the in-between times stitch those larger experiences together. Without them, the narrative would feel thin, stripped of texture. Maybe there’s a certain peace in paying attention to these transitions, to the moments when nothing is demanding urgency. When one appointment ends and another hasn’t begun, when a chapter of a book is finished and we haven’t yet turned the page. These spaces invite reflection without pressure. They allow the mind to wander sideways, to connect unrelated thoughts, to let the imagination meander. 

Consider, for instance, the familiar act of brewing tea. The kettle rises from cupboard to stove, water sloshing softly inside. There’s the waiting, that small window when steam first whispers about the spout. Some people use that time to tidy the counter. Others glance at their phones. But the kettle has its own rhythm, an in-frame stretch of time between the start of heat and the whistle. This simple moment isn’t particularly profound, yet it exists, filling countless minutes across countless days. Suppose we counted up every kettle’s lull, every bus platform pause, every stretch of corridor between meeting rooms. We might discover a significant portion of our living occurs between “important” events. So perhaps those implicit moments deserve more attention, more curiosity.

When we start to notice them, we may realize how they shape mood and perception, how they influence our sense of time. There’s a difference between a morning rush and a morning that allows five extra minutes to let the dog sniff hedges or to stand with a mug on the porch. Those five minutes are entirely in-between. Nothing grand happens there. Yet they can frame the mood for hours afterward, introducing either calm or irritation, expansion or constriction. Another person might find that the stretch between work and home—maybe a subway ride, maybe a walk through a crowded plaza—creates space for decompression. The commute is sometimes maligned as wasted time, but when we tweak our lens, it becomes a sanctuary for podcasts, for looking out windows, for letting thoughts breathe. It’s a transition that helps us shift contexts, stepping from one role to another. 

Similarly, creativity thrives in in-between times. So many ideas arrive not when we’re staring at a blank page or whiteboard, but when we’re folding laundry, gazing out the window, marching through a grocery aisle. By engaging in an activity that’s necessary but not mentally demanding, we open up a channel where fragments can assemble themselves. The brain can shuffle puzzle pieces without pressure. This speaks to the importance of idleness as well, of afternoons without plans, of roads taken without destination. When these “wasted” times are valued, we discover that they often contain the seeds of the ideas we later celebrate. 

People often talk about being “present,” but what does that really mean if not to inhabit the moment we’re in—even when the moment feels unremarkable? Cultivating attention to the mundane can almost feel like an act of quiet rebellion against the insistence on urgency that modern culture often demands. It’s a subtle refusal to treat every second as a rung on a ladder toward productivity. Instead, we inhabit those seconds simply to exist within them. There’s a liberating sense of autonomy in doing so, a small reclaiming of personal experience from the narratives that say only achievements or crises are worth our time. 

Sometimes the in-between periods are not just small slivers of time but longer stretches—weeks or months where we’re waiting for news, or trying to figure out what’s next. These liminal phases can be uncomfortable because they lack clarity. Yet they also invite introspection. They encourage experimentation, even if the experimentation is quiet or internal. Perhaps you pick up a half-forgotten hobby, or you wander through a library without a list, selecting whatever title surfaces. Maybe you notice the pattern of shadows on your wall or the hum of a refrigerator that’s been there all along. Attention starts to reconfigure the ordinary, revealing that our environments are richer than we usually allow ourselves to see. 

It’s funny how technology both expands and shrinks these in-between moments. On the one hand, a smartphone can dissolve liminal time completely; the minutes that once contained daydreams now fill with scrolling. On the other hand, technology can also help us memorialize and appreciate the ordinary—capturing a photo of sunlight through a window, sending a spontaneous note, listening to a song that becomes tied to a particular corridor or stretch of road. The key may be not to vilify the tools themselves but to use them mindfully, preserving the possibility that some intervals remain open, meditative, unfilled. It can be delightful to resist the impulse to fill every gap with stimulation, to let the silence hum untreated.

The notion of liminal space extends to physical environments, too. Stairwells, hallways, lobbies, and thresholds have a peculiar charm. They are designed to be passed through, not inhabited. Yet when architecture has a certain warmth or intrigue, those spaces become memorable. Think of an old theater lobby with ornate ceilings, or a stairwell painted with murals. These transitional places become characters in their own right. They support the experience even though they’re not the main attraction. The same applies to seasons that feel transitional—maybe a late autumn day, or early spring, when weather can’t decide whether to simmer or chill. The uncertainty of these seasons often leads to bursts of creativity in fashion, art, and even cooking, because we improvise to bridge the shift.

There’s a concept in anthropology called “liminality,” introduced by Arnold van Gennep and developed by Victor Turner. It refers to the middle stage of rites of passage, a threshold phase where participants are no longer who they were but not yet who they will be. During such phases, social structures loosen, norms bend, and possibilities open. The liminal is a place of transformation, but it can also be disorienting. Even in everyday life, the liminal carries a certain combination of vulnerability and potential. Maybe that’s why the in-between can feel unsettling; we haven’t yet anchored ourselves to what comes next. But it can also be exhilarating precisely because of that openness. The in-between offers us choice. Instead of being locked into a script, we’re free to redefine it.

To appreciate the in-between is to embrace patience. That doesn’t mean complacency. It means trusting that not all progress is linear or readily measurable. You can care deeply about your goals while acknowledging that the path toward them includes long stretches of gathering, learning, recalibrating, or just being. There’s something gentle about giving yourself permission to be “in process,” not just at arrival or departure. As a culture, we often emphasize endpoints—graduations, launches, releases, promotions. But each of those points is preceded by hours of practice, brainstorming sessions, drafts, false starts, sleepy mornings, and late-night cups of tea. To say yes to the in-between is to honor the whole arc, not merely the peaks.

It’s encouraging how many creative works lately celebrate the unremarkable. Films, essays, and photo diaries capture quiet rooms, small gestures, the soft clatter of utensils in a home kitchen. Instead of aiming for spectacle, they linger on small moments and trust the viewer to relate. When we see these portrayals, we feel recognized. Our own ordinary lives mirror back at us. We realize that others share the same kind of Tuesday afternoon boredom or Sunday morning ease. Rather than feeling isolated in our routine, we feel connected through it.

Memory is peculiar in this regard. Sometimes a day we considered “normal” ends up being the one we recall most vividly, maybe because of the way the sunlight hit the pavement or a random conversation that carried warmth or humor. The in-between moments are actually the infrastructure of our personal memories, quietly shaping who we are. Maybe that’s why traveling feels so special: it changes our daily in-between patterns. Suddenly, the morning walk is new, the grocery store is different, the coffee break features different scenery. We pay attention because everything is unfamiliar. But we can cultivate a similar attention at home by treating the familiar as if it were fresh, by asking ourselves to notice what we usually ignore.

The best stories often contain these quiet beats, too. Think of novels where the turning point happens not in a climactic showdown but in a simple conversation in a kitchen, or while two characters share a ride in silence. The ellipses between dramatic events often hold the emotional weight. Writers like to say that subtext lives in these spaces—the things unsaid but implied, the tension between what a character wants and what they reveal. Life mirrors this narrative technique. The subtext of our lives is often most apparent in the in-between, when our guard lowers, when we’re unobserved, when there’s room for our interior selves to surface.

So maybe the next time we find ourselves stuck in traffic or waiting in a line, we can experiment by paying attention not to the delay but to the scene as it unfolds, to the colors and patterns, to the snippets of overheard dialogue. Maybe we engage our senses, noticing the smell of rain on asphalt or the texture of a paperback in our hands. Perhaps we let our minds wander down a story idea or revisit a memory. The in-between doesn’t need to be merely endured. It can be a space of curiosity, comfort, and depth.

Ultimately, living fully in those interim moments might change how we view our sense of time altogether. Instead of seeing life as a succession of highlights separated by filler, we might recognize that the so-called filler is the majority of the experience, and it’s imbued with meaning if we allow it to be. By appreciating the subtle, the quiet, and the transitional, we anchor ourselves more deeply in the present. We acknowledge that the journey isn’t just a waiting room for destinations; it is the destination.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
